visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 338 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 31 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 34.9 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 347 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 32 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 29 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 36.7 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 365 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 34 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 30 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 37.6 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 374 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 31 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 27 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 38.5 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 383 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 32 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 30 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 41.2 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 410 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 27 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 42.1 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 419 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 32 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 28 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 43.0 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 428 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 33 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 29 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 43.9 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 437 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 31 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 27 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 45.7 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 455 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 32 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 28 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 46.6 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 464 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 33 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 29 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.

visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 34 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 30 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 48.4 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 31 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 27 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 49.3 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 32 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 28 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 508 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock

visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 33 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 29 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 526 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 31 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 27 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 535 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 32 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 28 mmHg and CPP 50 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 33 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 55.5 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 553 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 34 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 56.4 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 562 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?
rationale: Low filling pressures plus high compensatory SVR indicate a preload problem rather than vasodilatory or pump-failure shock. The body is constricting aggressively to maintain perfusion. This profile is most consistent with hypovolemia.
takeaway: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

visualRationale.title: Hemodynamics / Shock
visualRationale.caption: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low preload pressures + high SVR -> think hypovolemia.

stem: A patient has inferior STEMI changes with hypotension, bradycardia, and jugular venous distention. What is the priority action?

options.text: Support preload with cautious IV fluid while preparing reperfusion.
Delay intervention until right-sided ECG is completed.

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + JVD + hypotension -> RV infarct until proven otherwise.

stem: An ARDS patient has a plateau pressure of 31 cmH2O. What ventilator adjustment is the priority?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to improve ventilation.
Lower tidal volume to keep plateau pressure at or below 30 cmH2O.
Ignore plateau pressure if oxygenation is improving.

rationale: Plateau pressure above the lung-protective threshold increases volutrauma risk. Lowering tidal volume is the first-line ARDSnet response. The other options either increase harm or ignore the safety signal.
takeaway: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.
visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 57.3 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.

stem: A postoperative patient has MAP 571 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, and SVR 2100 dynes·s/cm5. Which shock state best fits?

rationale: This is classic right-ventricular infarct physiology. These patients are preload dependent, so nitrates can worsen the collapse. A cautious fluid bolus is the safer immediate support measure while definitive reperfusion is arranged.

visualRationale.conclusion: High plateau pressure means reduce stretch first.

stem: A TBI patient has ICP 28 mmHg and CPP 52 mmHg. What should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: Give hypertonic therapy per order and maintain head-neck alignment.

rationale: Elevated ICP with subgoal CPP requires prompt ICP reduction while preserving perfusion. Hypertonic therapy and neutral alignment directly support that goal. The other choices worsen cerebral physiology or delay needed treatment.
takeaway: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP + high ICP -> lower ICP without sacrificing perfusion.

stem: A patient with AKI has potassium 58.2 mEq/L and peaked T waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Wait for nephrology to arrive before treating.
Give sodium bicarbonate first and reassess later.

rationale: ECG changes from hyperkalemia indicate immediate membrane instability. IV calcium protects the myocardium within minutes and buys time for potassium-shifting and removal therapies. The other options delay the lifesaving first step.
takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> stabilize the heart first.
